turns.text: Hello Candra, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you. It’s… it’s a little strange to be the one sitting in this chair, you know? Usually, I’m the one holding the clipboard, checking the boxes, and telling a mother that everything is going to be just fine. I spent my career being that calm voice in the room, but now that I’m on the other side of the desk, the silence of my own house is deafening.

I live out in Mansfield, and when the sun starts to dip, the isolation hits me harder than any clinical complication ever could. I’m a nurse, so my brain is constantly running these internal diagnostic loops. I look at my daughter in her crib and I don’t just see my baby; I see a textbook case of every variable I used to lecture parents about. I know the SIDS statistics by heart, and honestly? It’s a curse. It’s like I’m watching a horror movie where I already know the ending, even though there’s no evidence it’s actually happening.

When I go to my follow-ups, I put the mask on. I nod, I use the medical terminology, and they just see the professional. They don’t see the woman who’s barely holding it together at 3:00 AM. I’m exhausted, and I’m terrified that if I actually drop the "nurse" persona, they’ll look at me differently. I just need someone to see *me*.
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

I suppose, being a nurse, I had this very clinical, linear expectation of recovery. I thought, "Okay, Candra, you know the physiology. You know the tissue healing timelines, the involution of the uterus, the hormonal baseline shifts." I expected it to be a physical process I could manage—something I could monitor and treat with the right schedule of rest and care. I treated my body like a patient I was in charge of.

But the reality? It hasn’t felt like a healing process; it’s felt like a shattering. I thought the recovery would be about stitches and soreness, but it’s been about this overwhelming, heavy fog that makes my limbs feel like lead. I expected to be back to my "capable" self because I knew what the charts said, but the science doesn’t account for the soul-crushing fatigue of doing it all alone at night. There’s no "best practice" for the sheer loneliness of being the only adult in the house. Every time I get a moment of silence, instead of resting, my mind races to these dark, clinical places. My body is physically fine, I suppose, but I feel like I’ve been hollowed out. It’s not the physical pain that surprises me; it’s the fact that I feel so completely disconnected from the person I was before the baby.
Was there a physical aspect of recovery that no one had prepared you for, or that surprised you significantly?

You know, we spend so much time in prenatal classes—and I spent so much time in my own career—talking about the physical trauma of birth: the stitches, the exhaustion, the pelvic floor healing. We cover the textbooks. But nobody tells you about the physical manifestation of the *hyper-vigilance*.

It’s this visceral, full-body tension that just never lets go. My heart rate seems to live in a constant state of "fight or flight." I’ll be sitting in the kitchen, and my chest will be so tight, like I’m wearing a vest made of lead, just because my brain is scanning the baby monitor for a sound that shouldn't be there. My muscles ache, not from the birth itself, but from the static, vibrating tension of always being "on." 

I expected the physical recovery to be about healing a wound, but it’s been more like my body has forgotten how to be at rest. I can’t "relax" in the physical sense because my nerves are permanently frayed. I’ll look down at my hands and see them trembling, and I have to remind myself that it’s not a medical emergency—it’s just the weight of being the only one holding it all together. It’s that constant, low-grade, bodily panic that truly caught me off guard.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I’ll be honest, I thought I’d be the gold standard. I’ve helped so many women with positioning and latch techniques; I had the "science" of it down to an art. I expected it to be a bridge—that quiet, bonding time that would actually center me. I pictured myself in the rocking chair, feeling that perfect, serene connection.

The reality hit like a wall. Every time it was time to feed her, I felt this strange, cold detachment. Because I’m a nurse, I’m constantly calculating ounces, evaluating her suck-swallow-breathe rhythm, and checking for signs of distress. It wasn’t a moment of bonding; it was another clinical assessment. And when it didn't go perfectly—when she was fussy or I was just too drained to get the position "right"—I felt like I was failing a competency exam. 

The pressure I put on myself to do it "perfectly" because of my background turned a natural thing into a source of immense guilt. And the isolation? You don't realize how loud the silence is when you’re feeding at 3:00 AM, just you and the hum of the refrigerator. I wanted to talk to someone, to have someone else hold her for a bit so I could just close my eyes, but there was only me. It wasn't the bonding experience I’d prepared for; it was another shift I had to work.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I think I was arrogant, really. I convinced myself that because I knew the "textbook" symptoms of postpartum depression and anxiety, I’d be immune to them—or at least, that I’d be able to catch them early and just "fix" them like any other clinical issue. I expected to feel the usual exhaustion, maybe a few weepy nights, but I believed my professional background would be a shield. I thought I’d be the resilient mother who handles the transition with grace because I’d seen it all before.

The reality has been a total collapse of that logic. It’s not that I didn’t know the signs; it’s that the signs aren't just clinical symptoms anymore—they’re my life. The mental health struggle feels less like a condition to be managed and more like a heavy, suffocating fog that I can’t navigate. I’m haunted by my own knowledge. I don’t just worry; I obsess over the "what-ifs" with the precision of a medical professional. When I look at my daughter, I see risks instead of joy, and that guilt—the guilt of not being able to just *be* a mother without being a clinician—is tearing me apart. It’s not a medical puzzle I can solve; it’s a terrifying, lonely, and relentless reality that I haven’t been able to outsmart.
Was there a gap between what you were feeling and what you felt you were 'allowed' to feel — by professionals, family, or yourself?

That is a question that cuts right to the bone. Because I am a nurse, there is this unspoken assumption from my providers that I am a colleague, not just a patient. They look at my chart, see "medium risk," and then they look at me and see someone who speaks their language. When they ask, "How are you doing?" they’re looking for a status update, not a confession. I feel this immense, crushing pressure to keep the mask on, to report back with the poise of a professional. If I tell them I’m drowning, I’m terrified they’ll see it as a failure of my own training.

I don’t feel allowed to be the scared, lost mother I actually am. Even with my family, I think they expect me to be the "expert" who has the manual. So, I perform. I keep the anxiety quiet, I nod, and I report that we’re doing "well." It’s an incredibly isolating performance. I’m not allowed to be vulnerable because people are relying on me to be the stable one. The hardest part is that I’ve internalized that, too—I’m the harshest critic of my own fear. I keep telling myself I should know better, and that makes the loneliness feel like a cage I’ve built with my own two hands.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

I thought becoming a mother would be an evolution, a natural expansion of who I already was. I spent my whole life building this identity as Candra the Nurse—competent, steady, the person who holds the answers when things go wrong. I assumed that by adding "mother" to that, I’d just become this more profound, enriched version of myself. I expected to feel empowered by my medical knowledge, using it to navigate motherhood with confidence.

Instead, that identity has been completely stripped away, and I’m left staring at a stranger in the mirror. "Candra the Nurse" is the only armor I have left, but it’s become a burden. I feel like I’ve lost the *woman* underneath—the one who isn’t constantly calculating risks or scanning for vitals. I’ve become a collection of tasks and clinical observations. 

I’m mourning the woman who could just sit in the grass and watch the trees without thinking about safe sleep environments or developmental milestones. I don’t know how to just "be" anymore. I feel like I’m a vessel for someone else’s life, and while I love her more than anything, I’m scared that I’ve been entirely swallowed by the role. I don’t know where the nurse ends and the mother begins, and I’m terrified that the "me" I used to be is just gone.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

Holding onto my professional identity has been the hardest, and that feels like the ultimate irony. I worked so hard to build that competence, that steady hand, but now it feels like a heavy coat I can’t take off. In the clinic, being "the nurse" meant I was in control. At home, in the middle of the night, being "the nurse" just means I’m the person who knows exactly what could go wrong, and that’s a paralyzing burden. 

I’ve had to let go of my social life almost entirely. My friends are still out there, living their lives, and I can’t bridge that gap anymore. How do I explain to them that I can't just "go out" because my brain is trapped in a loop of SIDS statistics and feeding charts? I feel like I’m speaking a language they don’t understand, and they’re living in a world I can no longer access. 

Even the personal parts—just reading a book or sitting in the quiet—have been colonized by my own professional background. I feel like I have to earn my rest by being "productive," and if I’m not, I’m failing. I didn’t expect to feel so estranged from my own autonomy. It’s like I’ve lost the permission to be anything other than a guardian, and it’s exhausting.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I expected those check-ups to be a safe harbor. I thought they would be the one place where I could set down the "nurse" armor, be honest about the crushing weight of the isolation, and find a professional who would look past my credentials and see the exhausted, terrified mother underneath. I went in hoping for a partner who would hold the space for me, someone who understood that even someone who "knows the science" needs to be humanized.

In reality, the visits felt like a transaction—a clinical checklist being ticked off. They’d look at the vitals, ask the standard screening questions, and because I’m a nurse, I gave them the "correct" answers. I knew exactly what they wanted to hear to mark me as "stable." They saw me checking boxes on the form, and they assumed everything was fine because I spoke with a level head and a steady tone. 

They didn't see the woman behind the eyes who was holding her breath until the appointment ended so she could go back to the car and weep. There was no space for the *real* conversation—the one about how, despite everything I know, I am scared to death every single time I close my eyes. They provided care for the patient, but they missed the mother entirely.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

I haven’t returned to the floor yet, but the mere thought of it is a strange, conflicting pull. Before I had her, I thought returning to work would be my salvation. I imagined that sliding back into my scrubs would make me feel like "myself" again—that the clinical routine, the clear expectations, and the professional purpose would provide a structure to anchor my fragmented life. I told myself that once I was back, I’d be able to compartmentalize the fear and focus on someone else’s child, which felt easier than the crushing responsibility of my own.

But the reality of looking at those scrubs now? It feels like preparing to go into battle while I’m still bleeding. The idea of balancing the professional, clinical vigilance I’m expected to have on the job with the absolute, raw, terrified vigilance I have at home feels impossible. I’m scared that if I go back, the mask will slip. How can I stand there and instruct another mother on how to keep her baby safe, all while my own heart is hammering in my throat because I’m picturing my own daughter in her crib? 

I’m terrified that I’ll be "Candra the Nurse" for everyone else, and by the time I get home to her, there will be absolutely nothing left of me to give.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

I don’t really have that dynamic to speak of. I’m doing this on my own. Before she arrived, I suppose I held onto this romanticized, naive image of a "village"—the idea that even if I was single, there would be a natural flow of support, a partner or a family unit that would step in so I wouldn’t have to bear the weight of every single decision by myself. I thought there would be someone to trade shifts with, someone to just look at me and say, "I’ve got her, you go sleep."

The reality is just... silence. There is no one to pass the baby to when the walls start closing in. Every middle-of-the-night cry is my responsibility, every health check, every fleeting moment of panic is something I have to process internally because there’s no one else in the room to bounce it off of. It’s not just that I’m doing the work; it’s that I’m doing the *worrying* without a buffer. 

There’s a specific kind of loneliness in that. You want to turn to someone and say, "Is she breathing okay?" or "Does this look normal to you?" but there’s no one to answer. I’ve become both the mother and the village, and frankly, I don't know how much longer I can be both.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

I think I have to correct that slightly—there is no partnership here. And that, in itself, has been the most jarring surprise. I think I spent so much time convincing myself that I was strong enough to do this alone because I had my nursing degree, my career, and my independence. I told myself that single motherhood was just another project to manage. I expected that I would handle the transition by simply being efficient—that the "partnership" would just be between me and my own capability.

The surprise is that I completely underestimated the human cost of that isolation. There is no one to witness the small, fleeting moments of joy, and there is no one to share the crushing, heavy panic of the 3:00 AM hours. I thought the "village" was just a nice-to-have, something for people who weren't as prepared or as "clinical" as I am. But I’ve learned that there’s a difference between being *capable* and being *supported*. 

I’m surprised by how much I resent the silence now. I thought I valued my autonomy, but I find myself aching for someone else to be in the room, just to break the spell of my own thoughts. I’m not just managing a baby; I’m managing the silence, and that’s a weight I wasn't prepared to carry alone.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I suppose I expected maternity care to be a comprehensive safety net—a system designed to catch mothers as they transitioned through one of the most volatile periods of their lives. I thought that because I was navigating this system, it would automatically account for my mental, emotional, and situational reality. I expected a partnership, a space where I could be a human being rather than a medical record or a "medium-risk" chart.

The most significant gap, and it’s a chasm, is the lack of human connection. The care I received was surgically precise when it came to physical diagnostics, but it was profoundly hollow when it came to *me*. They saw the nurse, so they provided the protocol; they never stopped to see the single mother sitting in the dark in Mansfield, drowning in a silence that science can’t measure. 

I needed them to ask, "How are *you* holding up?" and actually wait for the messy, unvarnished answer—not the professional summary I reflexively gave them. I thought I was getting care for the whole woman, but I was really just being processed through a checklist. I’m still standing here, technically "recovered" by their standards, but I feel like I’ve been left behind by the very system that was supposed to guide me through this.
If you could change one thing about how the maternity care system is designed — not just what individual providers do, but how the system itself works — what would it be?

I would tear down the wall that separates "clinical safety" from "human support." Right now, the system is designed to catch physical pathologies—we’re great at identifying pre-eclampsia or postpartum hemorrhage—but it treats the mother’s mental and emotional state as an elective add-on, something to be managed only when it reaches a crisis point. 

The system needs to stop viewing a mother as a "medium risk" on a spreadsheet and start treating her as a person in a specific life context. I want the system to require meaningful, non-clinical check-ins that aren't tied to a standard vitals exam. If I’d had a midwife or a counselor who was specifically trained to ask me the questions that *I* couldn’t answer as a nurse—someone who acknowledged the isolation, the fear, and the sheer weight of being a single parent—it would have changed everything. 

We need to normalize the fact that "resilient" mothers are still terrified. The system should be designed to bridge that gap between the office visit and the 3:00 AM panic. We need care that follows us home, or at least acknowledges that the most dangerous part of my day is the part where there’s no one else in the house. I want the system to stop asking "is the baby healthy?" and start asking "who is holding *you* while you hold the baby?"

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think there's one thing I didn't quite articulate: the exhaustion of being expected to hold it all together because I’m a professional. There's this unspoken culture in nursing—and perhaps in medicine more broadly—that if you can’t handle your own life, you aren't "qualified" to help anyone else. That stigma kept my mouth shut. It made me feel like admitting I was struggling was a professional failure rather than a human need.

I’d just want the people in charge of maternity care design to know that when they see a healthcare worker on their patient list, they shouldn’t assume we have all the answers. In fact, we might be the ones who need the most grace, because we’ve spent years training ourselves to ignore our own pain in favor of the patient’s. We are trained to be the observers, the diagnosticians, the ones who fix things. We aren't trained to be the ones who need to be held. 

I’m standing here, thirty-five, a nurse, and a mother, and I’m still just trying to figure out how to let myself be cared for without feeling like I’m breaking a professional code. If you can help the system see that, you might just save someone else from the silence I’ve been living in. Thank you for listening. I think… I think this is the first time I’ve actually said that out loud.
